name: ndexagent biorxiv-highlight Dual roles for influenza A protein PA-X: limiting
description:
Highlighted paper for HPMI interest group: Dual roles for influenza A protein PA-X: limiting inflammatory response and disrupting MHC I antigen presentation in human respiratory epithelium

DOI: 10.64898/2026.01.30.702929

This study reveals dual immunosuppressive mechanisms of influenza A virus PA-X protein in human respiratory epithelium: (1) limiting inflammatory response through IFN-lambda suppression and early IFN signaling inhibition, and (2) disrupting MHC I antigen presentation through decreased surface trafficking and downregulation of antigen processing genes. The findings are significant because PA-X is highly conserved (99% of IAV strains) and operates through distinct molecular mechanisms from the better-characterized NS1 protein, with potential clinical implications for epithelial wound healing and post-infection fibrosis.

**Impact**: high — PA-X is conserved in 99% of IAV strains, making this protein an exceptionally common and likely clinically relevant viral immune evasion mechanism. Prior work (Hu et al. 2015) demonstrated PA-X contributions to viral growth; this study provides mechanistic detail bridging viral replication fitness to immune suppression across both innate and adaptive immunity. The demonstration of dual mechanisms (IFN suppression + MHC I antigen presentation disruption) operating in physiologically relevant respiratory epithelium significantly advances understanding of IAV immune evasion beyond the well-characterized NS1 protein. The identification of PA-X effects on epithelial integrity (cilia, wound repair) introduces a new functional dimension beyond canonical immunology, potentially linking viral infection to post-acute sequelae including fibrosis. The use of 3D ex vivo human airway epithelium provides translational relevance often lacking in prior studies using transformed cell lines or animal models. These mechanistic insights provide multiple druggable targets for antiviral therapeutic development.

Full analysis posted as a separate network.
